original text:
From the earliest years of Summerside, many men and women from the outlying Acadian communities came here to find work.
They were employed as truckers, seamen, fishermen, farmers, carpenters, coopers, tinsmiths, stonemasons, maids, dressmakers, and washerwomen.
Through the years, they advanced to more lucrative and prestigious occupations.
Among Summerside's first Acadian entrepreneurs were Frank Perry (François Poirier) and his wife Annie Arsenault.
In 1899, they opened the popular Queen Hotel that served the community for half a century.
This 35-room hotel, described as "one of the most comfortable and homelike hotels in Maritime Canada," was located south of the Journal-Pioneer next to the railroad track, today the Confederation Trail.
Standing on the Queen Hotel's steps are Aubin E. Arsenault and Joseph Gaudet.
Aubin E. Arsenault (1870-1968) was Summerside's first Acadian lawyer.
He was also the first francophone elected to town council where he sat from 1906 until 1908, the year he was elected to the Legislative Assembly representing 3rd Prince.
In 1917, he became Premier of the province and in 1921 he was appointed to the Supreme Court of Prince Edward Island.
Joseph J. Gaudet (1880-1933), better known as "Joe Bunn", was born in Summerside.
As a young man, he worked as a trucker and then opened a local restaurant business.
In 1908, Gaudet operated one of the first movie theatres in Summerside, "The Happyland."
In 1922, "Joe Bunn" Gaudet opened the Capitol Theatre on Central Street.
Today, one third of Summerside's population claims Acadian ancestry.
The most common Acadian names in the city are Arsenault, Bernard, Blacquiere, Blanchard, Cormier, DesRoches, Doucette, Gallant, Gaudet, Perry (Poirier), Peters (Pitre), Richard, Sonier, and Wedge (Aucoin).
Facts: Mural # 8.
Measures 16 x 16 feet.
Erected November 2000 with funding from the Canada Millennium Partnership Program (CMPP).

original text:
On July 13, 2011, in his testimony before the House Financial Services Committee, Federal Reserve Chairman Ben Bernanke told Texas Congressman Ron Paul that he does not think gold is money, only an asset.
Mr. Paul pressed further and asked, "Why, then, do central banks hold gold as reserves?"
 Bernanke, looking a bit like a grade school student in a spelling bee contest that was just presented the word "incongruity," could only muster a one-word answer, "Tradition."
Apparently, these central bankers' monetary strategy is analogous to why Americans serve turkey on Thanksgiving.
Brilliant!
To be fair, given that Mr. Bernanke is the chief guardian and servant to the altar of fiat paper currency, a position of disregarding gold as money is to be expected, even if it ignores 6,000 years of history.
If you concur with Mr. Bernanke that, in fact, gold is not money and only an asset, you may want to stop reading here and move on to other things.
The perspective I will present is not good news, and conflicts with most of what conventional financial media tells you.
If, on the other hand, you disagree with Mr. Bernanke, or are at least open to another perspective on the markets, by all means, read on.
Call 855.753.8182 or email today.

original text:
We will explore the global high-end market for Japanese food.
The Japanese food industry is reputable even overseas with its high quality, safety and security.
The industry has been nurtured throughout Japan and there are lots of unique and hidden products with a long history in local cities.
Our aim is to educate the world about the culinary delights of Japan, even to those foreign consumers who have never tasted Japanese food.
It is the mission of Zelico to contribute delivering the delight of tasting Japanese food to people around the world.
We will convey the excellence of Japanese foods to nearly 200 countries.
Each country with its unique history, custom and natural resources has an individual food culture.
We will convey the excellence of Japanese food to people around the world while tailoring our products to each individual world market.
We are dedicated to increase the world' s recognition for the Japanese food culture while delivering an appreciation for Japanese food to people around the world.
This is what our primary vision is all about.
We aim to achieve sales to the value of \10 billion in the medium-to long-term through exporting Japanese food to nearly 200 countries.
2 Third ‐ country trade of the products produced under Japanese quality control.
3 Import of the products from all of the world.
Corporate Name Suzuki Eikodo Inc.
From the foundation in 1877, production of candies and jellies including "Zelico" .
Planning, purchasing, and selling of confectioneries to amusement parks and facilities.
Planning, importing, and selling of stuffed toys, key chains, and gifts to amusement parks and facilities.
1877 Founded as a confectioner in Tawara town, Oogaki city by Mr. Kamesaburo Suzuki.
The completion of the company building.
Started the production of jelly.
1933 Incorporated.
Mr. Denshichi Suzuki assumed the position of first president.
The company capitalized at 50 thousand yen.
1939 Started to export strawberry jelly to Hawaii.
1948 The first president, Mr. Denshichi Suzuki assumed the position of Oogaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry' s vice president.
1960 Increased its capital to 3 million yen.
The first president, Mr. Denshichi Suzuki won the Honorary President Award at the 16th National Confectionery Exposition for the whole of his great achievements in the industrial development of confectionery.
1961 Mr. Denshichi Suzuki won the Honorary President Award from National Small and Medium-sized Enterprises Association.
1971 Started the import of stuffed toy, metal/wooden toys and so forth.
1976 Mr. Seijiro Suzuki assumed the position of third president.
1989 Achieved sales of 1 billion yen.
2004 Built the second factory at Asakusa town, Oogaki city.
Built the new Yoro Distribution Center.
Increased the capital to 30 million yen.
Founded Eikodo Shanghai with 1 million CNY in capital.
Built the new jelly production factory at Iga city, Mie prefecture.
Founded EIKODO VIETNAM CO.LTD with 500,000 USD in capital.
Tokyo Dream Inc. became a wholly owned subsidiary company.

original text:
Comedy nights in the Granary Leith bar take place on the last Friday of every month.
It's a relatively new affair run by performer and compere Paul McDougall.
Due to the Leith festival taking place this week Paul ran an additional evening to coincide with other similar events taking place during the festival.
My partner and I decided initially to take advantage of the facilities and have some dinner in the Granary club itself.
If you have never been to the Granary then I highly recommend it, its setting on the Shore front creates a genteel vibe and unlike other smaller bars in the busy Leith circuit the large seating area and expansive bar help to create a more easy-going and relaxing feel to its contemporaries.
Upstairs in the gig, Paul carries his enthusiasm and charisma through his act by speaking to each audience member in turn, and having a chat with them whilst riffing some comedy on the answers he receives.
Often when comedians go down this route it's an opportunity for cruel gags at the audience's expense but Paul keeps it friendly, creating a homespun atmosphere which encourages a relaxed vibe throughout the gig.
Three acts followed before the headliner took to the stage.
Bruce Fummy introduced himself as the leading Afro-Celtic comedian on the comedy circuit before admitting he's actually in a field of one there.
Gentle routines about being a mixed race Scotsman were well received.
Jay Miles' sedate deadpan delivery has some very cunning one-liners, comedy with a lot of thought.
Thomas Black looks like a young Frankie Boyle and has some material that he could really work with to be a success.
The headliner is Jo Caulfield, as seen on TV shows regularly over recent years, which for a £3 admission fee, is just the bargain of a lifetime.
Jo is honing material for her Fringe show and I will certainly be there, her observations on relationships had all partners (including us!)
looking at each other with knowing smiles.
With other major acts scheduled for the months ahead this event deserves a wider audience.

original text:
Hop on a helicopter and enjoy a spectacular aerial view of the Coast Mountains.
Come see the famous backcountry of Whistler BC while soaring over mountains, lakes, volcanoes, and more!
And, for those who feel exceptionally brave, take an adventure with a Glacier Landing and explore a side of Whistler so few will ever see.
Book Blackcomb Helicopters Calendar Click here for quick booking!
Chevron down What will I see on a helicopter trip?
On the introductory helicopter trip, you'll explore the incredible surroundings of the Whistler area.
On the 24-minute trip, you'll enjoy a bird's eye view of the Whistler area while flying by the incredibly beautiful Cheakamus Glacier and Black Tusk.
Also included is a 15-minute glacial landing.
Chevron down Can I bring my kids along?
Yes!
If your kids are under the age of 2, they fly for free on a parent's lap.
Chevron down What happens if I book a trip on a stormy day?
All helicopter trips are subject to weather conditions, availability or aircraft, and civil emergencies.
If on the day of your trip the weather is determined to be unsafe to fly, your trip will be cancelled and no charges will apply.
Chevron down Do you provide shuttle service?

Free shuttle is available from Whistler Village to the Whistler Heliport for up to 14 passengers.
Chevron down Is there an additional charge for glacier landings?
Absolutely not!
Glacier landings are 100% complementary and dependent on weather conditions.
Chevron down How many people can go into the helicoptor per trip?
Helicopter seats max.
5 or 6 persons.

original text:
Monsoon is usually a stressful time for Leila Alvares, because it is when she starts readying her new musical.
This year, even more so.
Alvares was in Bengaluru, rehearsing for The Addams Family, when landslides hit her coffee estate and homestay near Madikeri in Kodagu.
She lost a few crops, but fortunately, her children were here with her, and none of the labourers working at her farms were affected.
She did think of driving back to Kodagu to help in the relief efforts, but the roads were broken and led nowhere.
She is still here, and is hoping that her new show runs houseful across Bengaluru, Mangaluru, and Payyanur, so she can donate the proceedings towards the rehabilitation of Kodagu.
The show at Kottayam in Kerala had to be cancelled due to the floods.
For the past 21 years, Alvares has been making musicals and donating the funds raised to charities working for the poor, stray animals, and the environment.
Only "20 per cent" goes back to The CAUSE (Cooperation of the Arts for the Underprivileged in Society & Environment) Foundation, a not-for-profit that she runs to promote local artists.
"We need that amount as a corpus for our next production because we struggle to get sponsorship," she admits.
This, despite the fact that all her cast and crew, which can total 36, (including her teenage son and daughter) work for free, purely out of love for the stage.
Just take the case of The Addams Family, which tells the story of a ghoulish American family.
Of the `15lakh budget for its Bengaluru leg, "only about four lakhs have come through sponsors", and the rest from advertisements and in kind.
So tickets sales are critical, and free passes are a big no until somebody has contributed to the show.
"One time, somebody called me up, claiming he was from the CM's office, asking for passes.
I just said 'Sorry, please buy your own ticket'," recollects Alvares, frank as always.
To ensure footfalls, she creates musicals that her audience is familiar with, such as The Sound of Music, Fiddler on the Roof, Young Frankenstein and Dirty Rotten Scoundrels.
That's how The Addams Family also made the cut.
It is based on the characters of the iconic cartoon series of the same name, which was later adapted to TV, films and Broadway.
"I was not a real fan of the TV series but I found the musical - its songs, dance sequences, and costumes - quite entertaining," she reasons.
In here, Wednesday Addams has fallen in love with a man her parents have never met, and then confides in her father and begs him not to tell her mother.
Now, Gomez Addams must do something he's never done before - keep a secret from his Morticia.
Everything will change for the whole family on the fateful night they host a dinner for Wednesday's 'normal' boyfriend and his parents.
"What makes this family popular is that even though they are vampire-like, they exhibit human qualities; they go through the same trials and tribulations that humans do," she says.
Besides The Addams Family, her first book also kept her on her feet this year.
Called Once upon a time, this 304-pager is part-autobiography, part-commentary on our times, and part-recommendations of her go-to music, books, and foods.
Again, some proceeds from the book sales, to be launched alongside her musical, will go towards the rehabilitation work.
